findings.description: The NC Energy, Mineral & Land Resources logo in the HTML rendering appears slightly different in positioning relative to the header text compared to the PDF.
findings.evidence: In PDF, the logo is positioned to the left with header officials' names to the right. In HTML, the logo appears but the layout relationship is slightly different though still functional.

findings.description: The document uses 'MEMORANDUM' as the h1 title, which is acceptable, but the section headings (I through IV) are rendered as h2, which is a reasonable structural choice. No significant hierarchy issues.
findings.evidence: h1: MEMORANDUM; h2: I. History/Background, II. March 1, 2016 Public Meeting and Oral Comments Summary, III. Written Public Comment Summary, IV. Attachments

findings.description: The page footer text is repeated five times throughout the HTML document, once per original PDF page. While the footer does appear on each page of the PDF, in a clean HTML conversion these repetitive footers should ideally be consolidated or appear once.
findings.evidence: The footer 'State of North Carolina | Environmental Quality | Energy, Mineral and Land Resources / 512 N. Salisbury Street | 1612 Mail Service Center | Raleigh, North Carolina 27699-1612 / 919 707 9200' appears five times in the HTML.

findings.description: The page header block (Governor, Secretary, Director names) from page 1 is rendered once, which is correct, but embedded images are duplicated — there are 5 img tags for what appears to be per-page decorative elements.
findings.evidence: Five

findings.description: The first image has a reasonable alt text ('NC Energy, Mineral and Land Resources logo'), but the remaining four images have empty alt text, which is generic/unhelpful. However, these may be decorative page-boundary artifacts.
findings.evidence: alt='NC Energy, Mineral and Land Resources logo' for first image; alt='' for remaining four images.

findings.description: The HTML has nested <main> tags, which is an HTML structural issue but does not affect content fidelity.
